content.text:
Now I have enough information to give you a comprehensive answer. Here's a summary of my findings:

---

## 1. Google Maps Reviews Scraper

**Recommended Actor:** `compass/google-maps-reviews-scraper` (reviews-only variant of the main `compass/crawler-google-places`)

The main Google Maps Scraper by Compass is `compass~crawler-google-places` (you already have this in your codebase at line 9 of `/Users/REDACTED_USER/Desktop/POC/FutureSight/reviewlens-ai/src/lib/scraper.ts`). There is also a dedicated reviews-only actor at `compass~google-maps-reviews-scraper`.

**Input JSON body:**
```json
{
  "startUrls": [
    "https://www.google.com/maps/place/Some+Business/@44.58,-110.51,9z/data=!3m1!4b1!4m5!..."
  ],
  "maxReviews": 100,
  "reviewsSort": "newest",
  "language": "en"
}
```

- `startUrls` -- array of Google Maps place URLs (full URLs, or shortened `maps.app.goo.gl` links)
- `maxReviews` -- integer, max reviews per place (default is very high; set to e.g. 100)
- `reviewsSort` -- one of `"newest"`, `"mostRelevant"`, `"highestRanking"`, `"lowestRanking"`
- `reviewsStartDate` -- optional, ISO date filter (e.g. `"2024-05-03T20:00:00"`)

**Output dataset item fields (per review):**

| Your need | Field name | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Review text | `text` | string |
| Translated text | `textTranslated` | string |
| Star rating | `stars` | number (1-5) |
| Date | `publishedAtDate` | string (ISO 8601, e.g. `"2025-09-05T04:56:59.605Z"`) |
| Reviewer name | `reviewerName` | string (requires `scrapeReviewerName: true`) |
| Review ID | `reviewId` | string |
| Review URL | `reviewUrl` | string |
| Owner response | `responseFromOwnerText` | string |
| Owner response date | `responseFromOwnerDate` | string |
| Reviewer photo | `reviewerPhotoUrl` | string |
| Reviewer review count | `reviewerNumberOfReviews` | number |
| Local guide? | `isLocalGuide` | boolean |
| Language | `language` | string |
| Like count | `likesCount` | number |

**Important:** Reviewer personal data fields (`reviewerName`, `reviewerUrl`, `reviewerId`, `reviewerPhotoUrl`) are disabled by default for GDPR. You need to enable them in the input (e.g. `"scrapeReviewerName": true`).

There is **no "verified" field** -- Google Maps reviews don't have a verified purchase concept like Amazon.

---

## 2. Yelp Reviews Scraper

**Recommended Actor:** `yin/yelp-scraper` (free, open-source on GitHub) or `delicious_zebu/yelp-reviews-scraper` (paid, more polished).

The open-source one (`yin~yelp-scraper`) has well-documented output fields from its [GitHub repo](https://github.com/yin/apify-yelp).

**Input JSON body (yin/yelp-scraper):**
```json
{
  "directUrls": [
    "https://www.yelp.com/biz/aces-pizza-brooklyn"
  ]
}
```

- `directUrls` -- array of Yelp business page URLs
- Alternative: use `searchTerm` for keyword search
- One of `searchTerm` or `directUrls` is required

**Input JSON body (delicious_zebu/yelp-reviews-scraper):**
```json
{
  "directUrls": [
    "https://www.yelp.com/biz/aces-pizza-brooklyn"
  ]
}
```

**Output dataset item fields (per review, from yin/yelp-scraper):**

| Your need | Field name | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Review text | `text` | string |
| Star rating | `rating` | string (e.g. "5") |
| Date | `date` | string |
| Reviewer name | `reviewerName` | string |
| Reviewer URL | `reviewerUrl` | string |
| Reviewer location | `reviewerLocation` | string |
| Reviewer review count | `reviewerReviewCount` | number |
| Photos | `photos` | string[] |
| Useful count | `isUsefulCount` | number |
| Funny count | `isFunnyCount` | number |
| Cool count | `isCoolCount` | number |

There is **no "verified" or "title" field** in Yelp reviews -- Yelp reviews don't have titles or verified purchase badges.

---

## Summary of Actor IDs (tilde format for API calls)

| Platform | Actor ID (tilde format) | Actor ID (slash format) |
|---|---|---|
| Google Maps | `compass~google-maps-reviews-scraper` | `compass/google-maps-reviews-scraper` |
| Google Maps (full scraper) | `compass~crawler-google-places` | `compass/crawler-google-places` |
| Yelp (free) | `yin~yelp-scraper` | `yin/yelp-scraper` |
| Yelp (paid, reviews-focused) | `delicious_zebu~yelp-reviews-scraper` | `delicious_zebu/yelp-reviews-scraper` |

The API endpoint pattern is: `POST https://api.apify.com/v2/acts/{ACTOR_ID}/runs?token={TOKEN}` with the JSON input as the request body -- exactly matching the pattern you already use in your scraper at `/Users/REDACTED_USER/Desktop/POC/FutureSight/reviewlens-ai/src/lib/scraper.ts`.

## Input JSON (startUrls)

The `startUrls` field is an optional array containing a list of URLs to be crawled, which can be search URLs, place URLs or review detail URLs. Valid Google Maps place URLs must contain one of the following subpaths: /maps/search, /maps/place or /maps/reviews.

Example input structure from the API documentation:

{
  "startUrls": [

    "https://maps.app.goo.gl/vErvoGd5JjKgbtkG8"
  ]
}
```

## Output Fields

The scraper extracts all reviews from Google Maps place URLs, including review text, date, rating, reviewer name and profile link, owner responses, and review URLs. Output includes review text, published date, response from owner, review URL, and reviewer's details.

Specifically, the output includes:
- **reviewText** - The text content of the review
- **rating** - The star rating given in the review
- Additional fields: published date, owner responses, review URLs, and reviewer details (name, profile link)

**maxReviews** (integer, Optional): Max number of reviews per place to scrape. If you fill in 0 or nothing, no reviews will be scraped. For all reviews, just put 99999. The default value of this property is 10000000.

# ReviewLens AI — Review Intelligence Portal

A secure, web-based portal that ingests product reviews from Amazon and enables analysts to interrogate that data through a **guardrailed AI Q&A interface** — without the AI ever drifting off-topic.

**Live URL:** _[deployed URL here]_

---

## Architecture

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Next.js 16 (App Router)                    │
│                    Deployed on AWS Amplify                    │
│                                                               │
│   ┌─────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   │
│   │  Ingest     │   │ Dashboard    │   │  Q&A Chat        │   │
│   │  URL+CSV    │   │ Stats+Charts │   │  Streaming Drawer│   │
│   └─────┬───────┘   └──────┬───────┘   └────────┬─────────┘   │
│         │                  │                    │             │
│   ┌─────▼──────────────────▼────────────────────▼──────────┐  │
│   │                Next.js API Routes                      │  │
│   │  POST /api/ingest  — scrape via Apify or parse CSV     │  │
│   │  POST /api/chat    — guardrailed Q&A (SSE streaming)   │  │
│   └───┬───────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┘  │
│       │                               │                       │
│  ┌────▼───────────┐    ┌──────────────▼───────────────────┐   │
│  │ Scraper        │    │ Amazon Bedrock                   │   │
│  │ Apify REST API │    │ ┌──────────────────────────┐     │   │
│  │ + Product page │    │ │ Bedrock Guardrails (L1)  │     │   │
│  │   fetch        │    │ │ Denied topic policies    │     │   │
│  └────────────────┘    │ └───────────┬──────────────┘     │   │
│                        │ ┌───────────▼───────────────┐    │   │
│  ┌────────────────┐    │ │ Claude Opus 4.6 (L2)      │    │   │
│  │ AWS Comprehend │    │ │ System prompt guard       │    │   │
│  │ (Sentiment)    │    │ └───────────────────────────┘    │   │
│  └────────────────┘    └──────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  SessionStorage (client-side session persistence)     │    │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Tech Stack

| Layer              | Technology                                        |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Framework          | Next.js 16.2 (App Router, TypeScript, Turbopack)  |
| Styling            | Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui                       |
| LLM                | Amazon Bedrock — Claude Opus 4.6                  |
| Scope Guard L1     | Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (denied topic policies) |
| Scope Guard L2     | System prompt engineering                         |
| Sentiment Analysis | Amazon Comprehend (BatchDetectSentiment)          |
| Scraping           | Apify REST API (Amazon Reviews Scraper)           |
| Overall Rating     | Direct product page fetch + regex extraction      |
| Import (fallback)  | CSV upload / paste                                |
| Charts             | Recharts                                          |
| Chat Rendering     | react-markdown with custom theme                  |
| Storage            | sessionStorage (client-side)                      |
| Deployment         | AWS Amplify                                       |

## Key Design Decision: Dual-Layer Scope Guard

The assignment emphasizes scope guard enforcement. Rather than relying solely on a system prompt, I implemented a **dual-layer approach**:

### Layer 1 — Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (Infrastructure-Level)

- Configured via AWS with **denied topic policies**: weather, news, politics, sports, other review platforms, general knowledge, competitor comparisons
- Applied to every Bedrock API call via `guardrailConfig`
- Blocks off-topic queries **before the LLM even processes them**
- Returns a structured intervention that the UI displays distinctly

### Layer 2 — System Prompt Engineering (Application-Level)

- Strict scoping: "You ONLY answer about the provided reviews"
- Citation requirement: responses must reference specific review numbers
- Graceful decline template for edge cases that slip past the guardrail
- Dynamic context injection: platform, product name, review count, date range

This is how you'd build scope enforcement in production — infrastructure-level guardrails for hard boundaries, prompt engineering for nuance and UX.

## Scraping Strategy

Two-tier approach:

1. **Apify REST API** (primary) — Pre-built Amazon Reviews Scraper actor (`junglee~amazon-reviews-scraper`), handles anti-bot, pagination, proxy rotation. Called via REST API directly (no SDK — avoids Turbopack dynamic import issues). Overall product rating and total global ratings are scraped in parallel from the product page.
2. **CSV Upload** (always works) — User pastes or uploads review data directly. Supports flexible column names.

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites

- Node.js 20+
- AWS account with Bedrock access (Claude Opus 4.6 enabled)
- AWS Comprehend access (BatchDetectSentiment permission)
- Apify account (free tier: 5 USD/month compute)

### Setup

```bash
# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/reviewlens-ai.git
cd reviewlens-ai

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy environment template
cp .env.example .env
```

### Configure Environment Variables

```env
# AWS (uses default credentials chain — ~/.aws/credentials or env vars)
AWS_REGION=us-east-1

# Bedrock
BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-6-v1
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_ID=your_guardrail_id
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_VERSION=DRAFT

# Apify
APIFY_API_TOKEN=your_apify_token
```

### Run Locally

```bash
npm run dev
# Open http://localhost:3000
```

### Run Tests

```bash
# Run all tests once
npm test

# Run tests in watch mode (re-runs on file changes)
npm run test:watch
```

Test suites cover the core business logic:

| Suite        | What it tests                                                          |
| ------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `csv-parser` | Column name variants, rating clamping, empty CSV, whitespace trimming  |
| `analytics`  | Rating distribution, averages, sentiment breakdown, keyword extraction |
| `prompts`    | System prompt construction, scope guard rules, decline template        |
| `store`      | Session CRUD, existence checks, overwrite behavior                     |

## Assumptions

1. **Amazon as target platform** — Most structured review data, well-supported by Apify scraper
2. **Client-side storage** — sessionStorage for prototype; production would use DynamoDB
3. **No user authentication** — Per assignment requirements, the app is directly accessible
4. **Bedrock costs** — Claude Opus 4.6 pay-per-token; total demo cost < $1
5. **Apify free tier** — 5 USD/month compute credit is sufficient for demo and evaluation
6. **English reviews only** — Comprehend sentiment analysis configured for English

## What I'd Do With More Time

- **DynamoDB persistence** — Sessions survive server restarts
- **Bedrock Agent + Knowledge Base** — RAG for 10,000+ reviews beyond context window limits
- **Multi-product comparison** — Compare sentiment across products
- **PDF/CSV export** — Download analysis reports
- **Rate limiting** — Protect the Bedrock API from abuse
- **Review pagination** — Handle products with 10,000+ reviews
- **E2E tests** — Playwright tests for the full flow

---

Built with Next.js 16, Amazon Bedrock (Claude Opus 4.6), Bedrock Guardrails, Amazon Comprehend, Apify, and Claude Code.

Powered by Mahmood.

# ReviewLens AI — Review Intelligence Portal

A secure, web-based portal that ingests product reviews from Amazon and enables analysts to interrogate that data through a **guardrailed AI Q&A interface** — without the AI ever drifting off-topic.

**Live URL:** _[deployed URL here]_

---

## Architecture

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Next.js 16 (App Router)                    │
│                    Deployed on AWS Amplify                    │
│                                                               │
│   ┌─────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   │
│   │  Ingest     │   │ Dashboard    │   │  Q&A Chat        │   │
│   │  URL+CSV    │   │ Stats+Charts │   │  Streaming Drawer│   │
│   └─────┬───────┘   └──────┬───────┘   └────────┬─────────┘   │
│         │                  │                    │             │
│   ┌─────▼──────────────────▼────────────────────▼──────────┐  │
│   │                Next.js API Routes                      │  │
│   │  POST /api/ingest  — scrape via Apify or parse CSV     │  │
│   │  POST /api/chat    — guardrailed Q&A (SSE streaming)   │  │
│   └───┬───────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┘  │
│       │                               │                       │
│  ┌────▼───────────┐    ┌──────────────▼───────────────────┐   │
│  │ Scraper        │    │ Amazon Bedrock                   │   │
│  │ Apify REST API │    │ ┌──────────────────────────┐     │   │
│  │ + Product page │    │ │ Bedrock Guardrails (L1)  │     │   │
│  │   fetch        │    │ │ Denied topic policies    │     │   │
│  └────────────────┘    │ └───────────┬──────────────┘     │   │
│                        │ ┌───────────▼───────────────┐    │   │
│  ┌────────────────┐    │ │ Claude Opus 4.6 (L2)      │    │   │
│  │ AWS Comprehend │    │ │ System prompt guard       │    │   │
│  │ (Sentiment)    │    │ └───────────────────────────┘    │   │
│  └────────────────┘    └──────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  SessionStorage (client-side session persistence)     │    │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Tech Stack

| Layer              | Technology                                        |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Framework          | Next.js 16.2 (App Router, TypeScript, Turbopack)  |
| Styling            | Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui                       |
| LLM                | Amazon Bedrock — Claude Opus 4.6                  |
| Scope Guard L1     | Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (denied topic policies) |
| Scope Guard L2     | System prompt engineering                         |
| Sentiment Analysis | Amazon Comprehend (BatchDetectSentiment)          |
| Scraping           | Apify REST API (Amazon Reviews Scraper)           |
| Overall Rating     | Direct product page fetch + regex extraction      |
| Import (fallback)  | CSV upload / paste                                |
| Charts             | Recharts                                          |
| Chat Rendering     | react-markdown with custom theme                  |
| Storage            | sessionStorage (client-side)                      |
| Deployment         | AWS Amplify                                       |

## Key Design Decision: Dual-Layer Scope Guard

The assignment emphasizes scope guard enforcement. Rather than relying solely on a system prompt, I implemented a **dual-layer approach**:

### Layer 1 — Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (Infrastructure-Level)

- Configured via AWS with **denied topic policies**: weather, news, politics, sports, other review platforms, general knowledge, competitor comparisons
- Applied to every Bedrock API call via `guardrailConfig`
- Blocks off-topic queries **before the LLM even processes them**
- Returns a structured intervention that the UI displays distinctly

### Layer 2 — System Prompt Engineering (Application-Level)

- Strict scoping: "You ONLY answer about the provided reviews"
- Citation requirement: responses must reference specific review numbers
- Graceful decline template for edge cases that slip past the guardrail
- Dynamic context injection: platform, product name, review count, date range

This is how you'd build scope enforcement in production — infrastructure-level guardrails for hard boundaries, prompt engineering for nuance and UX.

## Scraping Strategy

Two-tier approach:

1. **Apify REST API** (primary) — Pre-built Amazon Reviews Scraper actor (`junglee~amazon-reviews-scraper`), handles anti-bot, pagination, proxy rotation. Called via REST API directly (no SDK — avoids Turbopack dynamic import issues). Overall product rating and total global ratings are scraped in parallel from the product page.
2. **CSV Upload** (always works) — User pastes or uploads review data directly. Supports flexible column names.

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites

- Node.js 20+
- AWS account with Bedrock access (Claude Opus 4.6 enabled)
- AWS Comprehend access (BatchDetectSentiment permission)
- Apify account (free tier: 5 USD/month compute)

### Setup

```bash
# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/reviewlens-ai.git
cd reviewlens-ai

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy environment template
cp .env.example .env
```

### Configure Environment Variables

```env
# AWS (uses default credentials chain — ~/.aws/credentials or env vars)
AWS_REGION=us-east-1

# Bedrock
BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-6-v1
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_ID=your_guardrail_id
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_VERSION=DRAFT

# Apify
APIFY_API_TOKEN=your_apify_token
```

### Run Locally

```bash
npm run dev
# Open http://localhost:3000
```

### Run Tests

```bash
# Run all tests once
npm test

# Run tests in watch mode (re-runs on file changes)
npm run test:watch
```

Test suites cover the core business logic:

| Suite        | What it tests                                                          |
| ------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `csv-parser` | Column name variants, rating clamping, empty CSV, whitespace trimming  |
| `analytics`  | Rating distribution, averages, sentiment breakdown, keyword extraction |
| `prompts`    | System prompt construction, scope guard rules, decline template        |
| `store`      | Session CRUD, existence checks, overwrite behavior                     |

## Assumptions

1. **Amazon as target platform** — Most structured review data, well-supported by Apify scraper
2. **Client-side storage** — sessionStorage for prototype; production would use DynamoDB
3. **No user authentication** — Per assignment requirements, the app is directly accessible
4. **Bedrock costs** — Claude Opus 4.6 pay-per-token; total demo cost < $1
5. **Apify free tier** — 5 USD/month compute credit is sufficient for demo and evaluation
6. **English reviews only** — Comprehend sentiment analysis configured for English

## What I'd Do With More Time

- **DynamoDB persistence** — Sessions survive server restarts
- **Bedrock Agent + Knowledge Base** — RAG for 10,000+ reviews beyond context window limits
- **Multi-product comparison** — Compare sentiment across products
- **PDF/CSV export** — Download analysis reports
- **Rate limiting** — Protect the Bedrock API from abuse
- **Review pagination** — Handle products with 10,000+ reviews
- **E2E tests** — Playwright tests for the full flow

---

Built with Next.js 16, Amazon Bedrock (Claude Opus 4.6), Bedrock Guardrails, Amazon Comprehend, Apify, and Claude Code.

Powered by Mahmood.

│  └────────────────┘    └──────────────────────────────────┘   │

│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │

│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Tech Stack

| Layer              | Technology                                        |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Framework          | Next.js 16.2 (App Router, TypeScript, Turbopack)  |
| Styling            | Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui                       |
| LLM                | Amazon Bedrock — Claude Opus 4.6                  |
| Scope Guard L1     | Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (denied topic policies) |
| Scope Guard L2     | System prompt engineering                         |
| Sentiment Analysis | Amazon Comprehend (BatchDetectSentiment)          |
| Scraping           | Apify REST API (Amazon, Google Maps, Yelp)        |
| Overall Rating     | Direct product page fetch + regex extraction (Amazon) |
| Import (fallback)  | CSV upload / paste                                |
| Charts             | Recharts                                          |
| Chat Rendering     | react-markdown with custom theme                  |
| Storage            | sessionStorage (client-side)                      |
| Deployment         | AWS Amplify                                       |

## Key Design Decision: Dual-Layer Scope Guard

The assignment emphasizes scope guard enforcement. Rather than relying solely on a system prompt, I implemented a **dual-layer approach**:

### Layer 1 — Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (Infrastructure-Level)

- Configured via AWS with **denied topic policies**: weather, news, politics, sports, other review platforms, general knowledge, competitor comparisons
- Applied to every Bedrock API call via `guardrailConfig`
- Blocks off-topic queries **before the LLM even processes them**
- Returns a structured intervention that the UI displays distinctly

### Layer 2 — System Prompt Engineering (Application-Level)

- Strict scoping: "You ONLY answer about the provided reviews"
- Citation requirement: responses must reference specific review numbers
- Graceful decline template for edge cases that slip past the guardrail
- Dynamic context injection: platform, product name, review count, date range

This is how you'd build scope enforcement in production — infrastructure-level guardrails for hard boundaries, prompt engineering for nuance and UX.

## Scraping Strategy

Two-tier approach:

1. **Apify REST API** (primary) — Pre-built Amazon Reviews Scraper actor (`junglee~amazon-reviews-scraper`), handles anti-bot, pagination, proxy rotation. Called via REST API directly (no SDK — avoids Turbopack dynamic import issues). Overall product rating and total global ratings are scraped in parallel from the product page.
2. **CSV Upload** (always works) — User pastes or uploads review data directly. Supports flexible column names.

- Node.js 20+
- AWS account with Bedrock access (Claude Opus 4.6 enabled)
- AWS Comprehend access (BatchDetectSentiment permission)
- Apify account (free tier: 5 USD/month compute)

```bash
# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/reviewlens-ai.git
cd reviewlens-ai

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy environment template
cp .env.example .env
```

### Configure Environment Variables

```env
# AWS (uses default credentials chain — ~/.aws/credentials or env vars)
AWS_REGION=us-east-1

# Bedrock
BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-6-v1
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_ID=your_guardrail_id
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_VERSION=DRAFT

# Apify
APIFY_API_TOKEN=your_apify_token
```

### Run Locally

```bash
npm run dev
# Open http://localhost:3000
```

### Run Tests

```bash
# Run all tests once
npm test

# Run tests in watch mode (re-runs on file changes)
npm run test:watch
```

| ------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `csv-parser` | Column name variants, rating clamping, empty CSV, whitespace trimming  |
| `analytics`  | Rating distribution, averages, sentiment breakdown, keyword extraction |
| `prompts`    | System prompt construction, scope guard rules, decline template        |
| `store`      | Session CRUD, existence checks, overwrite behavior                     |

1. **Amazon as target platform** — Most structured review data, well-supported by Apify scraper
2. **Client-side storage** — sessionStorage for prototype; production would use DynamoDB
3. **No user authentication** — Per assignment requirements, the app is directly accessible
4. **Bedrock costs** — Claude Opus 4.6 pay-per-token; total demo cost < $1
5. **Apify free tier** — 5 USD/month compute credit is sufficient for demo and evaluation
6. **English reviews only** — Comprehend sentiment analysis configured for English

## What I'd Do With More Time

- **DynamoDB persistence** — Sessions survive server restarts
- **Bedrock Agent + Knowledge Base** — RAG for 10,000+ reviews beyond context window limits
- **Multi-product comparison** — Compare sentiment across products
- **PDF/CSV export** — Download analysis reports
- **Rate limiting** — Protect the Bedrock API from abuse
- **Review pagination** — Handle products with 10,000+ reviews
- **E2E tests** — Playwright tests for the full flow
